title: Fasting and Lent - Part 1
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Fasting and Lent - Part 1 - Matthew 6_16-18 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Why Study Fasting During Lent?
subpoints: Lent is the 40 days between Ash Wednesday and Resurrection Sunday
Lent is not a biblical festival and lacks clear scriptural warrant
The Bible does speak of fasting, making it worth our study
Jesus assumes His disciples will fast (Matthew 6:16 says 'when', not 'if')

outline.title: Three Acts of Righteousness: Giving, Praying, and Fasting
subpoints: Jesus addresses three acts prominent among the Pharisees in Matthew 6
These three acts correspond to the three relationships of life: others, God, and self
Jesus criticizes not the acts themselves but the wrong motivations behind them
The standard: our righteousness must exceed that of the Pharisees

outline.title: The Wrong Way to Fast: The Pharisees' Example
subpoints: Pharisees fasted twice a week (Mondays and Thursdays) and made a public show of it
They disfigured their faces and wore sackcloth to appear visibly pious
Their motivation was the praise of men, not the pleasure of God
When the praise of men is the goal, the praise of men is all the reward received

outline.title: The Right Way to Fast: Doing It Before God Alone
subpoints: Anoint your head and wash your face - simply go about normal life
The fast is between you and God, not a performance for others
Corporate fasting (as in Acts 13) is not excluded; the heart attitude is what matters
God who sees in secret will reward openly those who fast sincerely

outline.title: Complexities of Fasting: Questions to Consider
subpoints: The only commanded fast in Scripture is the Day of Atonement (Leviticus 16)
Paul warns against mandatory food restrictions as a sign of false teaching (1 Timothy 4:1-3)
Simply not eating can strengthen self-will rather than dependence on God
Is fasting distinctively a Christian practice, and in what sense does it apply under the new covenant?

outline.title: Biblical Examples of Fasting
subpoints: Jesus fasted 40 days in the wilderness but was not generally known as an ascetic
The prophetess Anna served God with fasting and prayer night and day
The Antioch church fasted before commissioning Paul and Barnabas
Paul references his own frequent fastings as part of his apostolic sufferings

summary:
In this first session of a Wednesday evening series on fasting, Pastor Tito Lyro introduces the topic in the context of the Western church calendar's season of Lent. While he does not regard Lent itself as a biblical observance, he uses the occasion to examine what the Bible actually teaches about fasting. The session centers on Matthew 6:16-18, where Jesus addresses fasting as one of three acts of righteousness - alongside giving and praying - that characterize the practical life of a disciple.

Pastor Lyro draws out a key structural point: these three acts correspond to life's three primary relationships (others, God, and self), and Jesus frames all three with the same concern about motivation. The Pharisees fasted publicly and visibly - deliberately disfiguring their faces to gain the admiration of men. Jesus says that when the praise of men is the goal, the praise of men is all one gets. By contrast, Jesus calls His disciples to fast in such an ordinary way that no one can tell they are fasting, keeping the discipline private between themselves and God.

The session also raises important questions about the theology of fasting. The only explicitly commanded fast in the Old Testament is the Day of Atonement (Leviticus 16), and Paul warns that mandatory food restrictions can be a mark of false teaching (1 Timothy 4:1-3). Simply abstaining from food, Lyro notes, can become an exercise in self-will rather than a spiritual act. Examples from the Old and New Testaments - including Jesus' 40-day fast, the prophetess Anna, and the Antioch elders - show that fasting was a regular part of God's people's experience without being universally commanded.

The session closes with the question left open for the following week: Is there such a thing as a distinctively Christian fast, and if so, what does it look like under the new covenant?
transcript:
## Introduction: Why Study Fasting During Lent?

We're going to be starting a short series — we'll see how many sessions it takes. On Wednesday nights — tonight, next week, and Lord willing the following week — we're going to consider the subject of fasting. And then following that, we're going to look at questions 173, 174, and 175 of the Larger Catechism, which have to do with how we prepare ourselves for the Lord's Supper, how we partake, what we should be doing as we partake, and what we should do afterwards as we go home. Those are two suggestions that were given to me, and I thought they made sense and would be helpful for everybody to think about.

So why fast? Why talk about fasting?

In church calendars in the Western world, this time of year is called Lent. Lent is the 40 days between Ash Wednesday and Resurrection Sunday. Those 40 days are supposed to represent the 40 days that Christ spent in the desert in Matthew 4 and Luke 4. Lent is supposed to be a period of grief and self-denial that often includes fasting, or giving up some luxury for the season. For some reason, chocolate is a popular thing to give up for Lent. Nick and I committed to give up kale. We're not eating kale during Lent. We're just sacrificing.

Now, Lent is not a biblical festival. It's not a biblical holy day. And I myself am not convinced of the propriety of observing Lent. It's a late tradition. Some Presbyterian churches — more of the high-church variety — do keep Lent, but I don't think we have the biblical warrant to establish this particular observance, especially when we connect two aspects of Jesus' life that are not necessarily connected other than being in the life of the same person: the 40 days in the desert and the resurrection.

Having said that, the Bible does speak of fasting. And so it is important for us to understand it. That's what really gave the impetus for this series. Jesus himself taught on fasting. Tonight we're going to look at Matthew chapter 6, verses 16 through 18. And as we read, you're going to see that this is one of those passages that at first seems so simple, and yet when you start actually thinking about what it says, you realize that the subject of fasting is much broader than simply not eating.

So Matthew chapter 6, verses 16 through 18. Jesus says:

> *"Moreover, when you fast, do not be like the hypocrites, with a sad countenance. For they disfigure their faces that they may appear to men to be fasting. Assuredly, I say to you, they have their reward. But you, when you fast, anoint your head and wash your face, so that you do not appear to men to be fasting, but to your Father who is in the secret place; and your Father who sees in secret will reward you openly."* (Matthew 6:16-18)

## Three Acts of Righteousness

This whole portion of the Sermon on the Mount — starting at verse 1 of chapter 6 — Jesus is addressing three areas of life that were central to the practical life of the righteous. If you were a righteous person, you'd be doing these three things Jesus lists. He picks them not because they are the three most important things a Christian can do, but because he's interacting with the practice of the time. At the time, if you were a Pharisee — considered the most righteous sect, the most righteous group of people around — you would pray, you would give alms, and you would fast. So he picks those three things.

The three acts are listed here in chapter 6, with fasting being the last. Notice in verse 1 of chapter 6 — and in the New King James it sounds a little different from what it should — it says, "Take heed that you do not do your charitable deeds before men, to be seen by men." The word *charitable* should actually be *righteous*. So be sure that you don't do your righteous deeds — giving, praying, and fasting — to be seen by men. That's not to be your motive, your impulse.

Jesus selects these three because they were the big ones with the Pharisees, the religious leaders he was interacting with throughout the Sermon on the Mount.

Now, there are several sects of Judaism in the first century. You have the Pharisees, the Sadducees, and then the Essenes, though you don't see them much in the New Testament. The Pharisees were the popular teachers — the ones who taught in the synagogues. The Sadducees were the priestly caste, usually upper class, who worked in the temple. Every high priest came from the Sadducees, not from the Pharisees. But the Pharisees were the popular ones, the ones people liked to follow, the example of righteousness. That's why Christ is interacting with their teaching. When Jesus says in the Sermon on the Mount, "You have heard it said" — he's not quoting the Old Testament. He's actually referring to the popular teaching of the time, which was often a misinterpretation of what God said in the Old Testament.

### Three Relationships

These three acts also highlight practical obedience in the three relationships that make up all of life. What are those three relationships? Think of the two greatest commandments. Love God — that's the relationship with God. Love your neighbor — that's others. And the second commandment says "as yourself" — that's self. So there are three relationships: self, God, and others. And you can see them in the three acts: giving relates to others, praying relates to God, and fasting relates to self.

Jesus warns his disciples to do their righteous deeds with the right heart. We see that in verse 1, and again in verses 3 and 4:

> *"But when you do a charitable deed, do not let your left hand know what your right hand is doing, that your charitable deed may be in secret; and your Father who sees in secret will Himself reward you openly."* (Matthew 6:3-4)

And again in verse 6:

> *"But you, when you pray, go into your room, and when you have shut your door, pray to your Father who is in the secret place; and your Father who sees in secret will reward you openly."* (Matthew 6:6)

So Jesus says that his disciples will practice these acts of righteousness with the motivation of pleasing the Lord.

Now, some of you might find this contradictory. We just prayed together as a congregation. We didn't go into a secret closet to pray. We take a public offering on Sunday. Jesus says to do these things in secret — not even letting your left hand know what your right hand is doing. Are we not following what Jesus commands here?

Remember that we have to take the whole Bible together. Jesus himself prays in public. He also prayed and fasted together with his disciples. He points out the widow giving her mite — he notices it publicly. This is not a manual saying you can only pray alone in a small closet, or only give so secretly that nobody can see. It's about the attitude of the heart. Don't do any of these things to be seen by men.

### Jesus Assumes His Disciples Will Fast

Also notice that in all three instances, Jesus assumes that his disciples will practice these acts. He doesn't say *if* you pray. He doesn't say *if* you fast. He doesn't say *if* you give. He says *when*. Verse 2: "Therefore *when* you do a charitable deed." Verse 5: "And *when* you pray." Verse 16: "*When* you fast." He assumes that his disciples will be doing these three things.

Notice also that Jesus nowhere criticizes the Pharisees for doing these things. He criticizes them for doing them wrongly. And he says your righteousness must exceed the righteousness of the Pharisees — they are doing it wrongly, you must do it right, with the right heart.

That leads us to conclude that we as disciples of Christ should be giving, praying, and fasting as well.

## The Wrong Way to Fast: The Pharisees' Example

Jesus goes on to expose the wrong way of fasting. Look at verse 16:

> *"Moreover, when you fast, do not be like the hypocrites, with a sad countenance. For they disfigure their faces that they may appear to men to be fasting. Assuredly, I say to you, they have their reward."* (Matthew 6:16)

The Pharisees of Jesus' time fasted twice a week. We know that from the parable in Luke 18, where the Pharisee prays and says, "God, I thank you that I am not like other men... I fast twice a week, I give tithes of all that I possess." The Didache — a second-century manual of Christian living — tells us that the Pharisees fasted every Monday and Thursday. And on their fasting days, the Pharisees attempted to look as miserable as they possibly could. They disfigured their faces to the point of being unrecognizable. They wore sackcloth and put ashes in their hair — they wanted to make sure people saw it. They didn't want to waste a fast.

Jesus tells us in verse 16 that they did all that to receive the praise of men, to be regarded by others as the righteous ones. They were slaves to men's opinion. The fear of men dominated their lives. They were ultimately concerned with men's approval, not God's.

And Jesus tells us: when we do what might be right with the exclusive intent of receiving the praise of men, then receiving the praise of men is all that we get. If that's what we're seeking, yes, we'll get it — but that's it. There's no glory ascribed to God. There's no eternal blessing. There's no smile from God upon us as we seek the approval of men. Even if we're doing something that is outwardly correct — if our intent is the praise of the people around us, then the praise of the people around us will be all the heaven we get. That's the point Jesus is making.

## The Right Way to Fast: Doing It Before God Alone

But he doesn't leave it there. He also tells us the right way of fasting. Verse 17:

> *"But you, when you fast, anoint your head and wash your face, so that you do not appear to men to be fasting, but to your Father who is in the secret place; and your Father who sees in secret will reward you openly."* (Matthew 6:17-18)

What is Jesus teaching here? When you fast — just go about life normally. Some interpret this passage as a call to be extra joyful when fasting, to go the opposite direction from the Pharisees. I don't think that's what Jesus means, because what would you be doing again if you did that? You'd be drawing attention to yourself again. All Jesus is saying is: don't make a big deal about it. Shower. Put on cologne or perfume as you would. Wear regular clothes as you normally do. Don't make it obvious that you're fasting. Fasting is between you and the Lord.

This doesn't mean that more than one person cannot fast together as a joint effort. The apostles did that. We see it in Acts 13: the Antioch elders gathered to commission Barnabas and Saul for the first missionary journey, and as they sought the guidance of the Holy Spirit, they fasted and prayed together. So again, it ultimately comes down to the attitude of the heart.

## Complexities of Fasting: Questions to Consider

By now you may be thinking — you said this passage is not as easy to understand as it looks. What's so hard to understand? Don't eat. Don't look miserable. Do it for the Lord. Three points, we're done.

Well, the difficulty is that there is very little instruction in the Bible about the purpose of fasting and how it is to be done. Jesus just says: when you fast, don't do like the Pharisees — and leaves it there.

Here it seems obvious that fasting means abstaining from food. But that is not clear in other passages in the Bible. And how does fasting fit with the new covenant and Paul's teaching concerning food? This is what Paul says in 1 Timothy 4:1-3:

> *"Now the Spirit expressly says that in latter times some will depart from the faith, giving heed to deceiving spirits and doctrines of demons, speaking lies in hypocrisy, having their own conscience seared with a hot iron, forbidding to marry, and commanding to abstain from foods which God created to be received with thanksgiving by those who believe and know the truth."* (1 Timothy 4:1-3)

There are two ways to interpret this passage. One is that this group was only forbidding people from eating certain foods — like some groups today that forbid shellfish. But there is also a very viable interpretation that this group was commanding mandatory fast days as part of their religious system. So it's not entirely clear that fasting simply means abstaining from food, and how that works with the rest of Scripture.

Also, simply abstaining from food is an exercise that can strengthen self-will — which is not necessarily something to be strengthened. It can just be a master's ability to not eat at certain times. Paul addresses this in the Colossian heresy — an early error in the church where people would come around telling you not to touch, not to taste, not to handle, practicing false humility and neglect of the body. Just not eating is not necessarily the same as biblical fasting.

### Where Does Fasting Come From?

Where does the idea of fasting come from? It's not part of human instinct — depriving oneself of food is not something you find naturally. Fasting is not essentially a Jewish or Christian thing. Buddhism and Hinduism practice fasting as well, and Hinduism is more ancient than Christianity or Mosaic Judaism.

Fasting is not even essentially a religious thing. Can you think of fasts that are not religious? Fasting before surgery. That's health-driven fasting. Have you heard of Mahatma Gandhi? One of the main tools he used in fighting for India's independence was fasting — political fasting, not religious. There's also the intermittent fasting movement for weight loss. And then there's fasting as manipulation — anorexia, for example, where fasting is designed to control the one thing in your life you feel you can control, or to control other people.

So fasting is not necessarily an exclusively religious thing. But though not essentially Jewish or Christian, fasting was part of the religious experience of God's people in the pages of Scripture.

## The Only Commanded Fast: The Day of Atonement

How many times do you think God's people are commanded to fast in the whole of the Bible — both Old and New Testaments?

In the Bible, God's people are commanded to fast exactly one time. And that is the Day of Atonement. That's the only command in all of Scripture for fasting. In Leviticus 16:29-31, most translations use the phrase "afflict your souls" — literally, in Hebrew, *to be bent over with hunger*. That is the idea behind "affliction of the soul" in that passage.

Now, why were God's people to fast on the Day of Atonement? What was happening? Remember there were a couple of goats, a lamb, an ox, several priests. The scapegoat had hands laid on it and was sent into the wilderness. The other goat was slaughtered, the lamb was slaughtered, the ox was slaughtered. The fasting on that day was a sign of repentance and mourning for sin — and of anxious anticipation as the high priest went into the Most Holy Place once a year to make atonement. Is God going to accept the sacrifice this year? Is the high priest going to come back out? They fasted in that anticipation, in that expectation of forgiveness.

But the Epistle to the Hebrews says this observance has been fulfilled in Christ. We no longer anxiously wonder whether our sins will be forgiven, because it is the blood of Christ that is upon the mercy seat. So we don't do the kind of fasting that was done on the Day of Atonement anymore.

## Fasting in the Life of God's People

Even though the Day of Atonement was the only commanded fast, fasting became the regular practice of God's people in the old covenant, especially following the Babylonian exile — not commanded, but regularly practiced.

In the New Testament, God's people fasted also, though we tend to find this practice earlier in the apostolic church — in the Gospels and early in Acts — more than in the epistles or later books.

The Lord Jesus himself fasted. There is only one recorded instance of his fasting: the 40 days in the wilderness. That's the only time recorded, though we don't know if that was the only time he fasted. As a matter of fact, Jesus was not known as an ascetic. He was not known as one who fasted all the time. Remember the contrast people made between him and John the Baptist? John comes eating only locusts and honey and they say he has a demon. Jesus comes eating and drinking and they say he is a glutton and a drunkard. So which is it?

There are other examples of fasting: the prophetess Anna served God with fasting and prayer night and day (Luke 2). The disciples fasted between the crucifixion and the resurrection — this is the implication of Matthew 9, which we'll look at next time. Cornelius was described as a God-fearing man who fasted and prayed, and the Lord came to him. In Acts 10, the Antioch elders fasted and prayed as they commissioned Barnabas and Saul for the mission field. Paul says he fasted while appointing elders in every church. Married couples in 1 Corinthians 7:5 may set apart time for fasting. And Paul himself says he fasted quite often, as you can see in 2 Corinthians.

## Closing Question

But the question still remains: are these examples normative for us to follow? In other words, is there such a thing as a Christian fast?

I'm going to end here tonight. The answer has actually already been implied in what we've looked at tonight, but we'll come back to pick it up again next Wednesday. Think about the things that were said tonight and see if you can figure out how that question was already answered.
